g.data-narration: Welcome to Reinforcement Learning - a powerful paradigm where agents learn through interaction and experience.
Unlike supervised learning from labels or unsupervised learning from patterns, RL learns through trial-and-error with rewards.
RL powers amazing achievements like AlphaGo, autonomous robots, and self-driving cars through continuous learning.
The RL system has two main parts: the Agent that learns and makes decisions, and the Environment that provides feedback.
g.text: Agent: Policy, Value Function, Learning Algorithm Environment: States, Actions, Rewards, Transitions

g.data-narration: The RL interaction loop: observe state, take action, receive reward, update knowledge, repeat until optimal.
The return G is the cumulative sum of rewards from time t onward. We maximize long-term returns, not just immediate rewards.
g.text: Sum of all future rewards from time t

g.data-narration: The discount factor γ (gamma) ensures finite returns by weighting future rewards less than immediate ones, with values between 0 and 1.
Trial-and-error is the heart of RL: agents explore actions, receive feedback through rewards, and gradually learn optimal behavior.
The exploration-exploitation dilemma: agents must balance trying new actions against using known good actions for optimal learning.
g.text: Example: A robot navigating a maze must balance
finding shorter paths vs taking known routes to food

g.data-narration: Epsilon-greedy is simple: with probability ε explore randomly, otherwise exploit the best-known action. ε decreases over time.
g.text: ε is typically decreased over time

g.data-narration: Beyond epsilon-greedy: Softmax selects probabilistically by value, UCB balances reward and uncertainty, Thompson sampling uses Bayesian approaches.
Value functions estimate how good states are: V(s) for state-value, Q(s,a) for action-value, guiding the agent's decisions.
g.text: Expected return starting from state s,
Expected return from state s, taking
Value of a state = expected value of all actions weighted by policy

g.data-narration: The Bellman equation expresses value recursively: V(s) equals immediate reward plus discounted future values, forming the foundation of RL algorithms.
g.text: Value of best action = immediate reward + discounted future value
Foundation for many RL algorithms including

g.data-narration: RL powers real-world applications: game playing, robotics, autonomous vehicles, resource management, finance, and healthcare.
g.text: Also: Resource management, Network routing, Recommendation systems

g.data-narration: In summary, RL involves agents learning through interaction with environments, maximizing cumulative rewards through trial-and-error with policy optimization.
